comprehensive_summary:
Katherine Mansfield (born Kathleen Mansfield Beauchamp, also known as Kathleen Mansfield Murry) was a New Zealand-born modernist short story writer who is overwhelmingly identified in quizbowl through her story "The Garden Party." In that story, Laura Sheridan oversees preparations for her family's garden party, including directing the placement of a marquee and receiving cream puffs from Godber's bakery. The story opens with the line "And after all the weather was ideal." Laura's sister Jose plays the song "This Life is Weary" on the piano while the maid Sadie requests sandwich flags. When Godber's delivery man brings news that the neighboring carter Mr. Scott has been killed by his horse, Laura tries to cancel the party, but her mother Mrs. Sheridan insists it go on. Laura feels ashamed of her fancy black hat trimmed with gold daisies when she delivers a basket of leftover food to the grieving Scott family. Upon seeing Scott's "wonderful, beautiful" corpse, described as "given up to his dream," Laura sobs and stammers "Isn't life-" to her brother Laurie, who responds "Isn't it, darling?" The band at the party wears green coats and is described as "too like frogs for words" by Kitty Maitland. The story also features the character Laura previously appearing in "Her First Ball."

Mansfield's story "Bliss" centers on Bertha Young, who feels an overwhelming sense of bliss symbolized by a pear tree in her garden that stretches up "like the flame of a candle." She contemplates the tree with her guest Pearl Fulton, with whom she shares a moment of "unearthly light, understanding each other perfectly." The guests include the Norman Knights (one dressed in an orange coat with monkeys), the poet Eddie Warren (who complains about a taxi driver and praises a poem beginning "Why must it always be tomato soup?"), and her husband Harry. At the end, Bertha discovers Harry is having an affair with Pearl Fulton, but the pear tree remains "as lovely as ever and as full of flower." Bertha insists on feeding her baby, Little B, herself, upsetting her nanny.

"Miss Brill" depicts a lonely English teacher who sits on a park bench at the Jardins Publiques wearing her beloved fur, which she imagines crying. She describes her breathing as "light and sad" before correcting herself to "gentle." A young couple mocks her fur, comparing it to a "fried whiting," and the story ends with her thinking "she heard something crying" as she puts the fur away.

"The Doll's House" features the recurring Burnell family and centers on a dollhouse given by old Mrs. Hay. The shunned Kelvey children (whose father is in prison and who wear clothes made from tablecloths and curtains) are ostracized by the other children. At the end, Kezia lets the Kelvey girls see the dollhouse, and Else whispers "I seen the little lamp."

"The Daughters of the Late Colonel" follows sisters Constantia (Con) and Josephine (Jug) coping with their father's death. Nurse Andrews annoys them by taking excessive helpings of bread and butter. They discuss giving their father's watch to their nephew Cyril, debate whether to fire the maid Kate, and realize they no longer need to silence the barrel organ that always annoyed their father, since he is dead. One sister stares at a mantelpiece Buddha statue.

"The Fly" features "the boss," a businessman whose son died in Belgium during World War One. He is reminded of the death by a visit from Woodifield, and proceeds to drop blobs of ink on a fly, killing it.

"Prelude" (adapted from the novel The Aloe) follows the Burnell family, including sisters Kezia and Lottie trying to fit into a luggage-packed buggy. A character chops off a duck's head with a tomahawk to demonstrate it can walk headless. Linda Fairfield realizes that life is "laughable, simply laughable" while observing an aloe. The story ends with Beryl Fairfield rejecting the advances of drunken Harry Kember. Kezia is laughed at for requesting strawberries and cream.

"How Pearl Button Was Kidnapped" describes a girl taken by "blue men" (or "two dark women") to the beach, where she has an enjoyable day before being carried back to the "House of Boxes." The character Pearl Button was inspired by Mansfield's relationship with Maata Mahupuku.

Biographically, Mansfield was born in New Zealand, was the inspiration for the character Gudrun in D.H. Lawrence's Women in Love, and possibly caught fatal tuberculosis from her close friend Lawrence. Her husband was John Middleton Murry, who published her work in journals like Adelphi. She was friends with Virginia Woolf. She died of tuberculosis at age 34. Claire Harman's biography All Sorts of Lives defends her against charges of plagiarizing Chekhov's story "Sleepy." She joined George Gurdjieff's Institute for the Harmonious Development of Man shortly after publishing The Garden Party and Other Stories. Janet Frame's mother worked as a maid for Mansfield's family. Eudora Welty suggested Mansfield wrote her regional stories out of homesickness while "eleven thousand miles from home." In Bharati Mukherjee's The Tiger's Daughter, the protagonist Tara writes her dissertation on Mansfield at Vassar. Mansfield described her stories as a "cry against corruption" in a letter to her husband, and wrote "How hard it is to escape from places" because "you leave little bits of yourself fluttering on the fences." Sandra Gilbert and Susan Gubar suggested in No Man's Land that Scott's death was inspired by the death of Mansfield's brother.

works.name: The Garden Party
works.description: Mansfield's most frequently clued work by far, appearing in virtually every question about her. It follows Laura Sheridan as she prepares for her family's garden party, learns of neighbor Mr. Scott's death from Godber's delivery man, tries to cancel the party, and ultimately delivers leftover food to the grieving family. The story opens with "And after all the weather was ideal" and ends with Laura stammering "Isn't life-" to her brother Laurie.

clues.clue: Laura stammers "Isn't life-" (or "Isn't life-isn't life-") to her brother Laurie at the end
examples: Laura stammers "Isn't life-" in a story by, for 10 points, what New Zealand-born author of "The Garden Party"?
That story by this author concludes with Laura asking "isn't life-?" to her brother Laurie Sheridan.

clues.clue: Laura delivers a basket of leftover food/cream puffs to the family of the deceased Mr. Scott
examples: After speaking to Godber's man, a character in a story by this author brings a basket of leftovers to the family of the deceased worker Scott.

In that story by this author, the protagonist is told to place a marquee 'bang slap in the eye.' Godber's man delivers cream puffs in a story by this author about a girl who carries a basket of leftovers to a newly widowed woman.

clues.clue: Mr. Scott is killed (by his horse / in a carting accident), which prompts Laura to try to cancel the party
examples: News of the carter Scott being killed interrupts the title event organized by Laura Sheridan.
The protagonist of a story by this author learns from a Godber's man that her neighbor Mr. Scott was killed, leading her to try to cancel the title event.

clues.clue: Godber's man/delivery man brings news of the death (and delivers cream puffs)
examples: A man from Godber's is received by a character who rehearses 'This Life Is Weary'
The protagonist is told by a Godber's man that her neighbor Mr. Scott was killed

clues.clue: Jose plays the song "This Life is Weary" on the piano; Sadie requests sandwich flags
examples: While a girl in a story by this author sings 'This Life is Weary,' the maid Sadie comes in to ask for sandwich flags.
The servant Sadie requests sandwich flags as Jose plays the song 'This Life is Weary' on the piano.

clues.clue: Opens with "And after all the weather was ideal"
examples: The sentence 'And after all the weather was ideal' begins a story by this author.
On a day where 'after all, the weather was ideal,' a character appeared in this author's story 'Her First Ball'.

clues.clue: Laura supervises/directs the placement of a marquee (told to put it "bang slap in the eye")
examples: The protagonist is told to place a marquee 'bang slap in the eye.'
That story opens with the protagonist directing the placement of a marquee.

clues.clue: Laura feels ashamed of her black hat trimmed with gold daisies; says "pardon my hat"
examples: The sobbing protagonist apologizes for wearing a 'black hat trimmed with gold daisies'.
One of this author's characters says 'pardon my hat' before fleeing a house.

clues.clue: Laura holds a piece of bread and butter while supervising workmen; eventually takes a big bite
examples: A central character in one story by this author first regrets bringing a slice of bread with butter outside, but eventually takes a big bite.
This character holds a piece of bread and butter while supervising the hanging of a marquee by four workmen.

clues.clue: The dead Scott is described as "wonderful, beautiful" and "given up to his dream"
examples: A character sobs and flees a house after seeing a man 'fast asleep,' 'given up to his dream.'
This author wrote about a girl who apologizes for her hat after seeing a 'wonderful, beautiful' sleeping face.

clues.clue: Mrs. Sheridan orders many canna lilies and insists on hosting despite Laura's pleas
examples: A woman orders a large number of canna lilies for an event which her daughter tries to cancel.
Mrs. Sheridan insists on hosting the title event despite Laura's pleas to cancel it.

clues.clue: The band wears green coats and is described as "too like frogs for words" by Kitty Maitland
examples: This group of people is described as 'too like frogs for words' by Kitty Maitland, due to their green coats.

clues.clue: Laura's mother comments on awful combination of eggs and olives; forgets sandwich flags
examples: A mother comments on the awful combination of eggs and olives after forgetting to bring 'sandwich flags.'

clues.clue: Sandra Gilbert and Susan Gubar suggest in No Man's Land that the corpse was inspired by the death of Mansfield's brother
examples: The theory that the corpse in 'The Garden Party' was inspired by the death of Mansfield's brother is suggested in No Man's Land.

clues.clue: Laura condemns the class distinction between herself and a workman who smells a sprig of lavender
examples: The protagonist of a story by this author condemns the class distinction between her and a workman who smells a sprig of lavender.

works.name: Bliss
works.description: A frequently clued story about Bertha Young, who experiences overwhelming bliss symbolized by a pear tree in her garden. She contemplates the tree with Pearl Fulton, only to discover at the end that her husband Harry is having an affair with Fulton. The story features the poet Eddie Warren and the Norman Knights among the dinner guests.

clues.clue: Bertha Young discovers her husband Harry is having an affair with Pearl Fulton
examples: This author created the characters Harry Young and Pearl Fulton, who are having an affair together, which ruins Bertha Young's happiness.
Bertha Young waits for Miss Fulton to 'give the sign' and stands with her in the moonlight, only to find out she is having an affair with her husband Harry.

clues.clue: The pear tree stretches up "like the flame of a candle" and at the end is "as lovely as ever and as full of flower"
examples: That poem features in a story by this author that describes a pear tree that stretches up 'like the flame of a candle.'
In the final line of this story, a pear tree is claimed to be 'as lovely as ever and as full of flower.'

clues.clue: Eddie Warren's poem begins "Why must it always be tomato soup?" / "nothing so eternal as tomato soup"
examples: Eddie claims 'Tomato soup is so dreadfully eternal.'
Those characters praise a poem beginning 'Why must it always be tomato soup?'

clues.clue: Bertha and Pearl stand together "caught in that circle of unearthly light, understanding each other perfectly" with "silver flowers" dropping
examples: Two women stand together 'caught in that circle of unearthly light, understanding each other perfectly,' with metaphorical 'silver flowers' dropping from their hair and hands.

clues.clue: Bertha hides her excitement "like a rare, rare fiddle" and insists on feeding her baby Little B herself, upsetting the nanny
examples: The protagonist is frustrated at having to keep her excitement hidden 'like a rare, rare fiddle', and her nanny is upset when she insists on feeding her baby, Little B, herself.

clues.clue: Norman Knights among guests; one wears an orange coat decorated with monkeys
examples: Characters include a woman dressed in an orange coat decorated with monkeys and a playwright who complains about a taxi-driver who sped up when asked to stop.

works.name: Miss Brill
works.description: A story about a lonely English teacher who wears her beloved fur to a park bench at the Jardins Publiques. A young couple mocks her, comparing her fur to a "fried whiting," and the story ends with her thinking she hears something crying as she puts the fur away.

clues.clue: A young couple on a park bench insults her fur; the boy compares it to a "fried whiting"
examples: A couple on a park bench insults the furs worn by the title English teacher.
Her fur is compared to a 'fried whiting.'

clues.clue: At the end, she thinks "she heard something crying" after putting away her fur
examples: One of this author's characters '[hears] something crying' after going home when a couple makes fun of her extravagant fur coat.
This story ends with a woman thinking 'she heard something crying' after putting away a fur coat.

clues.clue: She imagines her furs crying / the title character imagines her furs are alive
examples: The title character imagines her furs crying.

clues.clue: She sits at the Jardins Publiques; she notes "the band sounded louder and gayer" from her "special seat"
examples: This author wrote a story that opens with a woman in the Jardins Publiques who just bought new fur.
Another author from this country wrote a story whose narrator notes that 'the band sounded louder and gayer' while sitting in her 'special seat.'

clues.clue: She is an English teacher who censors her own thoughts, describing her breathing as "light and sad" then correcting to "gentle"
examples: An English teacher censors her own thoughts by describing her breathing first as 'light and sad,' then as 'gentle.'

clues.clue: A man rejects an "ermine toque" before young lovers ask "why doesn't she keep her silly old mug at home?"
examples: A teacher watches a man reject an 'ermine toque' before young lovers come to her park bench and ask 'why doesn't she keep her silly old mug at home?'

works.name: The Daughters of the Late Colonel
works.description: A story about sisters Constantia (Con) and Josephine (Jug) coping with their father's death. They deal with the annoying Nurse Andrews, debate whether to fire the maid Kate, and consider giving their father's watch to nephew Cyril.

clues.clue: Sisters Constantia and Josephine cope with their father's death
examples: Nurse Andrews and the maid Kate annoy Constantia and Josephine after their father's death.
Josephine and Constantia are the title characters of that story by this author.

clues.clue: Nurse Andrews takes excessive helpings of bread and butter
examples: Nurse Andrews takes excessive helpings of bread and butter in a story by this author about Con and Jug dealing with their father's death.

clues.clue: They realize they no longer need to silence the barrel organ that always annoyed their father
examples: Two women realize they do not need to silence a barrel organ because the man who had always been annoyed by it was dead.
A woman stares at a mantelpiece Buddha statue while listening to a barrel-organ that often annoyed a man who thumped his stick.

clues.clue: They discuss giving a watch to nephew Cyril and debate whether to fire the maid Kate
examples: Constantia and Josephine discuss giving a watch to Cyril and debate whether to fire their maid Kate.
Cyril is repeatedly asked if his father is fond of meringue by his aunts, who consider firing the chef Kate.

clues.clue: Constantia stares at a mantelpiece Buddha statue
examples: A woman stares at a mantelpiece Buddha statue while listening to a barrel-organ.

works.name: The Doll's House
works.description: Features the recurring Burnell family. A dollhouse is given by old Mrs. Hay, and the shunned Kelvey children are ostracized for their poverty. At the end, Kezia lets the Kelveys see the dollhouse, and Else whispers "I seen the little lamp."

clues.clue: Else whispers "I seen the little lamp" after Kezia lets the Kelvey children see the dollhouse
examples: Else whispers 'I seen the little lamp' after Kezia lets her see the title object in her backyard.
At the end of a story by this author, two sisters admiring the title object are chased out of a backyard, after which one says, 'I seen the little lamp.'

clues.clue: The Kelvey children are shunned because their father is in prison; they wear clothes made from tablecloths and curtains
examples: A little girl taunts two classmates for their father being in prison and ostracizes them for wearing clothes made out of a tablecloth and curtains.

clues.clue: Features the recurring Burnell family; the dollhouse is a gift from old Mrs. Hay
examples: This author's story 'The Doll's House' features the recurring Burnell family.
A story by this author about the old Mrs. Hay's gift to the Burnell family.

works.name: The Fly
works.description: A story about "the boss," a businessman whose son died in Belgium during World War One. After being reminded of his loss by a visit from Woodifield, he drops blobs of ink on a fly, killing it.

clues.clue: "The boss" drops blobs of ink on a fly, killing it
examples: In a story by this author, 'the boss' drops blobs of ink to kill the title fly.
The narrator tortures and kills a fly with blotting ink.

clues.clue: The boss's son died in Belgium during WWI; he is reminded by a visit from Woodifield
examples: The protagonist notes the unnatural expression in a photograph of his dead son, who he is reminded of upon a visit from Woodifield.
A businessman whose son died in World War One callously kills an insect using three drops of ink.

works.name: Prelude / The Aloe / At the Bay
works.description: "Prelude" follows the Burnell family and was adapted from the novel The Aloe. It features Kezia and Lottie, a duck beheading, Linda's realization about life, and Beryl rejecting Harry Kember. "At the Bay" continues the Burnell family saga.

clues.clue: A character chops off a duck's head with a tomahawk to prove it can walk headless
examples: A character chops off a duck's head with a tomahawk to prove that it can walk after being beheaded.

clues.clue: The novel The Aloe adapts "Prelude"; opens with sisters Kezia and Lottie trying to fit in a luggage-packed buggy
examples: This author's novel The Aloe adapts that story, which opens with the sisters Kezia and Lottie trying to fit into a buggy packed with luggage.

clues.clue: Linda Fairfield realizes life is "laughable, simply laughable" while observing an aloe
examples: Linda realizes that life is 'laughable, simply laughable' while observing an aloe in her garden.

clues.clue: Beryl Fairfield rejects the advances of drunken Harry Kember; Kezia is laughed at for requesting strawberries and cream
examples: A story by this author takes place entirely over one day that ends with Beryl Fairfield rejecting the advances of a drunken Harry Kember.
The protagonist is laughed at for requesting strawberries and cream while waiting for a buggy.

works.name: How Pearl Button Was Kidnapped
works.description: An early story about a girl taken by Maori women to the beach. She has an enjoyable day before being carried back by "little blue men" to the "House of Boxes." The character was inspired by Mansfield's relationship with Maata Mahupuku.

clues.clue: Pearl Button is taken by "blue men" or dark women to the beach; returned to "House of Boxes"
examples: In an early story by this author, a crowd of 'blue men' surround a girl at the beach after she spends an enjoyable day being kidnapped.
In a short story by an author from this country, the protagonist gazes at a woman's 'moonbeam fingers' as a crowd of 'little blue men' force her to return to the House of Boxes.

clues.clue: The child screams "will it hurt us—is it coming?" when she sees the ocean
examples: A small child screams and asks a 'big woman' 'will it hurt us-is it coming?' when she sees the ocean.

clues.clue: Inspired by Mansfield's relationship with Maata Mahupuku
examples: This author's relationship with Maata Mahupuku inspired an unfinished novella and several of her later short stories.

works.name: Other Works
works.description: Several minor works appear once or twice in the clues, including "The Canary" (about a lonely woman and a birdcage nail), "Mr. and Mrs. Dove" (Reggie wonders if he and Anne could be like two doves), "Sun and Moon" (a boy cries when his sister takes a nut off an ice pudding), "The Voyage" (Grandma and Fenella take a boat to Picton after her daughter-in-law's death), "Her First Ball" (a girl dances with young men who praise the floor quality, then an old man upsets her), "A Dill Pickle" (a man recalls a companion saying "Geranium, marigold, and verbena"), "Life of Ma Parker" (the title character has "nowhere" to cry after leaving a literary gentleman's flat), "Marriage a la Mode" (Isabel reads friends a love letter from her husband William), and "Something Childish and Other Stories."

clues.clue: "The Canary": lonely woman points to a nail that held the cage of a bird bought from a Chinese man
examples: A lonely woman points to a 'big nail to the right of the front door,' which once held the cage of a precious bird she bought from a Chinese man.

clues.clue: "Mr. and Mrs. Dove": Reggie wonders if he and Anne could be like the two title birds; "Roo-coo-coo-coo"
examples: A cry of 'Roo-coo-coo-coo' prompts Reggie to wonder if he and Anne could be like the two title birds.

clues.clue: "Sun and Moon": a boy cries when his sister takes a nut doorknob off an ice pudding
examples: A young boy bursts into tears after his sister takes a nut that was acting as a doorknob off of an ice pudding.

clues.clue: "The Voyage": Grandma explains death was "God's will" on the boat to Picton with granddaughter Fenella
examples: Grandma explains that the death of her daughter-in-law 'was God's will' while on the boat to Picton with her granddaughter, Fenella.

clues.clue: "Her First Ball": a girl dances with young men who remark on the floor quality; an old man tells her no one will want to kiss her when she's old
examples: A girl dances with young men who remark on the quality of the floor before being upset by an old man telling her no one will want to kiss her once she's old.

clues.clue: "Life of Ma Parker": ends with Ma Parker realizing "there was nowhere" for her to cry after leaving a literary gentleman's flat to mourn her grandson's death
examples: A story by this author ends with the title character realizing 'there was nowhere' for her to cry after leaving the flat of a 'literary gentleman' to mourn her grandson's death.

clues.clue: "Marriage a la Mode": Isabel feels guilt reading friends a love letter from her husband William
examples: Isabel feels a surge of guilt after she reads her friends a passionate love letter from her husband William.

clues.clue: Characters in a story discuss plays titled Love in False Teeth and Stomach Trouble
examples: Characters in a story by this author discuss plays titled Love in False Teeth and Stomach Trouble.

clues.clue: "A Dill Pickle": a man recalls a companion saying "Geranium, marigold, and verbena"
examples: In the short story 'A Dill Pickle,' a man recalls his companion saying the phrase 'Geranium, marigold, and verbena.'

clues.clue: In a story ("Something Childish"), a telegram "spans a web of darkness over the cottage" Henry rents with Edna
examples: A 'girl with a pinafore' passes Henry a telegram that 'spans a web of darkness over the cottage' he rents with Edna.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Katherine Mansfield was a New Zealand-born modernist short story writer. Key biographical facts that appear in clues include her connection to D.H. Lawrence (she inspired the character Gudrun in Women in Love and possibly caught tuberculosis from him), her marriage to John Middleton Murry, her friendship with Virginia Woolf, her death from tuberculosis at 34, and her association with Gurdjieff's Institute.

clues.clue: Born in New Zealand
examples: For 10 points, name this author who was born in New Zealand.
For 10 points, what New Zealand modernist short story writer wrote 'The Garden Party'?

clues.clue: Inspired the character Gudrun in D.H. Lawrence's Women in Love; possibly caught fatal tuberculosis from Lawrence
examples: This author, the inspiration for Gudrun in Women in Love, possibly caught fatal tuberculosis from her close friend D.H. Lawrence.
This author inspired the character of Gudrun in Women in Love after meeting DH Lawrence.

clues.clue: Husband was John Middleton Murry, who published her work in journals like Adelphi
examples: This partner of John Middleton Murry ends with the protagonist stammering 'Isn't life...'
This author inspired the character of Gudrun in Women in Love after meeting DH Lawrence via their husband, who published this author's work in journals like Adelphi.

clues.clue: Died of tuberculosis at age 34
examples: This author of 'Miss Brill' died of tuberculosis at 34.

clues.clue: Joined George Gurdjieff's Institute for the Harmonious Development of Man
examples: D.H. Lawrence was displeased when this author joined Gurdjieff's Institute for the Harmonious Development of Man.

clues.clue: Friends with Virginia Woolf; may have inspired Woolf to write "Kew Gardens"
examples: Woolf may have been inspired to write 'Kew Gardens' by this friend of hers.
After moving to the U.K., Mansfield became friends with this other female author.

clues.clue: Claire Harman's biography All Sorts of Lives defends her against charges of plagiarizing Chekhov's "Sleepy"
examples: Claire Harman's biography All Sorts of Lives defends this author against charges of plagiarizing Chekhov's story 'Sleepy' during a hospital stay in Bad Worishofen.

clues.clue: Described her stories as a "cry against corruption" in a letter to her husband
examples: This New Zealand-born author described her stories as a 'cry against corruption' in a letter to her husband.

clues.clue: Wrote "How hard it is to escape from places" because "you leave little bits of yourself fluttering on the fences"
examples: This writer observed 'How hard it is to escape from places,' because in doing so 'you leave little bits of yourself fluttering on the fences.'

clues.clue: Janet Frame's mother was a maid for Mansfield's family
examples: Janet Frame, was the daughter of a maid who worked for the family of this other New Zealand author.

clues.clue: In Bharati Mukherjee's The Tiger's Daughter, protagonist Tara writes dissertation on Mansfield at Vassar
examples: In Bharati Mukherjee's The Tiger's Daughter, the protagonist, Tara, writes her dissertation at Vassar on this author.

clues.clue: Eudora Welty suggested Mansfield wrote regional stories out of homesickness, "eleven thousand miles from home"
examples: Welty suggests that it was while she was 'eleven thousand miles from home, out of homesickness' that this author wrote regional stories.

clues.clue: Claimed to have written a possibly-lost novel about first muse Martha Grace
examples: This author claimed to have written a possibly-lost novel about their first muse, Martha Grace.

Author: described her stories as a "cry against corruption" in a letter to her husband
Short Story: a lonely woman points to a "big nail to the right of the front door" that once held the cage of a bird bought from a Chinese man
Short Story: "Roo-coo-coo-coo" prompts Reggie to wonder if he and Anne could be like the two title birds
Short Story: a boy bursts into tears when his sister takes a nut that was a doorknob off an ice pudding
Short Story: Grandma explains death was "God's will" on the boat to Picton with her granddaughter Fenella
Short Story: a girl dances with young men who remark on the floor quality before an old man upsets her by saying no one will kiss her when she's old
Short Story: the title character realizes "there was nowhere" for her to cry after leaving a "literary gentleman's" flat to mourn her grandson's death
Short Story: Isabel feels guilt after reading friends a passionate love letter from her husband William

summary: Katherine Mansfield (born Kathleen Mansfield Beauchamp, also known as Kathleen Mansfield Murry) was a New Zealand-born modernist short story writer who is overwhelmingly identified in quizbowl through her story "The Garden Party." In that story, Laura Sheridan oversees preparations for her family's garden party, including directing the placement of a marquee and receiving cream puffs from Godber's bakery.
